metadata.title: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = localized packet / initial; CRIMSON = spread packet / later
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no Gaussian-integral derivation, no chirp/complex-width algebra, no phase-vs-group recap.

beats.narration_text: A baseball thrown in a straight line keeps its shape. A quantum particle released as a tidy packet inevitably smears wider — and the tighter you pin it, the faster it blurs.

card.copy: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own

beats.narration_text: A localized quantum particle is not a single pure frequency — it is a bundle of many momenta, each with its own wave. The narrow the packet, the wider the spread of momenta inside it.
The packet starts tight. After some time it has smeared across a much wider region. No collision, no interaction — the particle just spread. How does it blur on its own?

card.copy: A tidy quantum packet. No forces. No collisions. It still spreads wider with time. How does a particle blur on its own?

beats.narration_text: For a free particle, energy scales as k squared — higher momentum, faster speed. The fast components in the packet race ahead; the slow components fall behind.
As time passes, the fast waves pull ahead and the slow waves lag. The packet stretches because its Fourier components all travel at different speeds.
The spread grows with time. And the more tightly localized the original packet, the wider its momentum spread, so the faster the fast components run away. Tight start, fast spreading.

This is the same reason a drum beat sounds sharp up close but echoes far away — each partial travels at its speed and the group loses coherence. For quantum mechanics, the time scale for doubling the width goes as sigma-zero squared times mass over h-bar.

This spreading is not disorder or heat — it is purely the quantum dispersion relation. A photon in vacuum doesn't spread this way because its dispersion is linear, not quadratic. An electron's quadratic dispersion is what makes it blur.

Illustrative example: an electron pinned to 1 nanometer at time zero. Its width doubles in about 0.3 femtoseconds — roughly the time it takes light to cross an atom. Tighten the pin to 0.1 nanometers and the doubling time shrinks to 3 attoseconds.
A localized particle is a bundle of momenta. High momentum moves faster. The bundle spreads. The tighter you started, the faster you spread.

card.copy: Localized = bundle of momenta. High momentum moves faster. The bundle spreads. Tighter start: faster spread.

beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Foundations: The Quantum World series.
